summary: I was born in Iowa in 1918, and my early years were shaped by the rural and community life there. Growing up in Iowa, my family was deeply involved in the Free Methodist Church, which influenced my upbringing. It was a place where family and faith were central to daily life before I moved away during World War II.

My family was involved in the Free Methodist Church while I was growing up in Iowa, and it played an important role in our community and values. The church was a foundation for us, providing a sense of belonging and moral guidance during my childhood in Waterloo. It was part of the fabric of my early life before I left for California during the war.

I grew up in Waterloo, Iowa, where my father worked as an ironworker and I eventually followed in his footsteps. It was a close-knit community, and my family was active in the Free Methodist Church there. Waterloo was where I learned the trade and values that I carried with me when I moved to California during World War II.

During World War II, I moved to Richmond, California, to work at the Kaiser Shipyards, which were booming at the time. Richmond was a bustling place with a huge demand for workers, and I experienced intense work schedules and strong camaraderie among the shipyard workers. After the war, Richmond changed a lot, especially during the redevelopment in the 1960s, which affected the downtown area and the community deeply.

World War II was the turning point that brought me from Iowa to Richmond, California, to work at the Kaiser Shipyards. The war effort created a massive demand for shipbuilding, and the work was intense and highly supervised. It was also a time when women and minorities entered the workforce in new roles, changing the dynamics of the trade significantly.

I worked at the Kaiser Shipyards in Richmond during World War II, where the pace was demanding but the sense of camaraderie was strong. The shipyards were a hub of activity, employing a diverse workforce including women welders who were new to the trade but did excellent work. It was a challenging environment with long hours and strict supervision, but it was also rewarding and full of community spirit.

I worked alongside a diverse group of ironworkers on the Richmond–San Rafael Bridge project during the war years. This project was part of the broader industrial boom in Richmond, and it brought together many workers from different backgrounds. The bridge stands as a reminder of the hard work and cooperation that defined that era in Richmond.

I have many fond memories of working on the waterfront, including fishing trips with the Ghio brothers. They were part of the community of workers at the shipyards and the waterfront, and those times helped build strong bonds among us despite the tough work. The Ghio brothers were good friends and part of the fabric of life in Richmond during and after the war.

The 1960s were a difficult time for Richmond, especially with the redevelopment that led to the loss of the downtown district. Many people felt anger and sadness as the city lost its center, and the changes affected the community deeply. Despite this, efforts to rebuild and attract new businesses began, marking a new chapter in Richmond's history after the war years.
